sentences.text: So now I'm concentrating to find my sister.
And I'm with the Primacs.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and one day, I went out to a park there.
And it was close-- near a river.
And it was a nice, sunny day.
And I just sat down there, you know, just resting, whatever-- looking at the trees.
And I said to myself, well, I'm going to give time till the war is finished.
And-- and when the war is finished, and I can't find my sister by then, I'm going to come back here and throw myself into the river.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember the river was so calm and so beautiful.
And the sky was so blue.
And I said, I'll just wait till the war is finished.
And at that time, I looked up at the sky.
And it was sort of like I felt like God's talking to me.
And-- but instead, I started to talk to God.
And I promised God if I find my sister alive, of course, I'll fast three days and three nights in a row with no food, no water, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to thank him for that.
If I should not be able to do that for some reason, I shall never go to Palestine, which is Israel, of course, now.
At that time, it was Palestine.
And so I made that promise to God that that's what I'll do if he helps me find my sister.
Somehow, I got up, and I went-- I went back home to the Primacs.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I would do some work there, too.
You know, I helped them with dishes, whatever.
I would help.
In Korzec.
This was in Korzec.
I guess I did change my clothing, finally.
Yes.
And-- yeah, they had food.
And right away, Mr.
Primac, he went-- he was doing business already on the market.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I wasn't hungry there.
I mean, I wasn't-- I just wasn't-- I didn't feel hungry.
And then I would think of my sister and just get very sick.
And one day, I was up with a very high fever, and I couldn't get up from the chair.
And my legs were giving under me.
The Primacs were very nice, and they called a doctor.
They found a doctor, which wasn't easy.
And this doctor says, oh, I have to stay in bed for about four days-- not to move, not to get down.
I don't know what it was.
He put me on some medication.
And sure enough, after three or four days, I did get better.
Now, I'm going back to the market and looking for my sister to see if-- if she'll if she'll come out, you know.
If she'll-- I can find her.
No, wasn't there.
And then one day, Mr.
Primac came home.
And he says to me, you know, somebody told him-- it was always like, somebody told him-- that there was a woman at the market, and she was a Czechoslovakian farmer.
And she says that she has a little girl-- a  young, little girl, a Jewish girl-- that's with her.
So that's what this person told Mr.
Primac.
And he tells me.
And I says, well, where are those farmers?
Where are those Czechoslovakian farmers?
He didn't know.
The women didn't know.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, could have been all the other girls-- survivors.
And one morning, again, I got up very early.
I got dressed.
And I told the Primacs, I says, I'm going now to look for my sister.
And they said, well, do you know where to go?
I says, well, I'll just hitchhike and go and look for her.
So they wished me good luck.
And they told me that if I found her, I can come back with her to their place.
So here I went to the highway and started the hitchhiking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And sure enough, a truck stopped.
It was a man and a lady, and they had a young child with them.
And they told me to jump up in the truck-- in the open truck there.
And I told the farmer-- like, I think somebody told me where there's some Czechoslovakian farmers are like-- I can't remember how many kilometers.
You know, I don't know, but he told me how far I should go, or whatever.
And I told this farmer, will you stop at so and so village-- whatever I told him, I don't know.
On the highway, I told him, I'll let-- I'll let you know where.
And he said, OK.
Or I could have told him where to stop, so I can get off and look for farmers.
And he says, OK, he'll stop there.
And we're driving, and we're driving, driving.
And I sort of-- I thought-- like I say, I can't remember how many kilometers it was.
Maybe 100, 120, 130, I don't know.
And I thought, this should be-- he should be stopping here.
He's not stopping.
And I'm knocking on the window, you know, to-- he should see me.
And he is driving away.
He is driving away.
And I thought, oh, God.
Where am I going?
You know, you missed the place already.
And finally, I-- still knocking and screaming for him to stop.
And the child noticed me and, I guess, motioned to the father and mother to stop.
And the farmer stops.
And he leaves me off-- left me off on this highway near a village.
And I thanked him.
And then I start to walk again to go from house to house to see if there's a young girl there.
And I come to this house, and I walked into the house.
And they were eating lunch, and it smelled so good, you know, whatever they had.
I was hungry already, because it was like, maybe, late in the afternoon.
And I asked them, are there any Czechoslovakian farmers around here?
And they said, they don't know.
They don't know them, but they-- they heard of some Czechoslovakian farmers on the other side of the highway, east of the highway, that possibly there are some.
And I said, oh, can you tell me exactly where to go or how far to go?
So the farmer said, yeah, I'll take you up to the highway.
I'll show you which way to cross the highway and where to go.
In the meantime, they asked me if I want to eat.
They must have noticed me because I looked at their table, and it looked so good you know.
They asked me, are you hungry?
I says, yes.
And so they gave me some food there.
Again, I wouldn't eat the bacon.
Every-- every farm there had bacon.
I wouldn't eat the bacon.
I would just eat around it.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I thanked them.
And the farmer showed me-- took me to the highway.
And I crossed the highway.
And it's sort of getting a little-- sunset-- it's getting a little darker, but still light enough to walk for me and look.
And I walked, and I walked and I see this big haystack, maybe-- again, a farm with hay, with grain, and all that.
And I thought, well, maybe I should stop here.
Whatever, I'm going to stop and ask if there's any Czechoslovakian farmers here.
And as I approach this-- this haystack-- whatever it was-- and I came around it, and I see this little girl with her back.
She's sort of was with her back standing.
And she was busy taking the hay or the grain, whatever, there.
And I thought, turn around so I can see you.
And she turns around.
[SOBBING] And there was my sister.
I said, Sonya!
And she says Rachela!
And it was just a miracle-- a miracle from God.
And you know, we just hugged each other.
Couldn't believe it.
I just couldn't believe it, that this-- that she's alive, and I found her here.
And then, the lady of the house saw us, so she came out, and she says, oh, this must be your sister.
This must be your sister.
I said, yes, yes.
And she was a Czechoslovakian farmer, and this was the Czechoslovakian farm.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just-- I was happy and sad and.
And, you know, it was just-- you know, God helped me to find her.
So we stayed over the night.
You know, the lady was very nice.
She says, I know you want her.
You want her back in the city now and all that.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the next day-- You went back?
Yeah.
I took her--
I came back to Korzec-- hitchhiking again, now.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And see, like, I think it was probably a bigger shock for my sister to see me, because I knew I was looking for her.
I think she was in shock, sort of, like, couldn't believe it, that-- that here I am, that it's me.
She looked so frail and so-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, so-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] but she was alive.
And that, to me, was the main thing.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
I went back to Korzec and didn't go to the Primacs right away.
Because I think what happened-- my sister wasn't feeling good.
And it was still a long way to go to the Primacs' house.
So again, some Jewish people were there, and they told me we can stay there, you know, till my sister feels better.
Then my sister, the next day, got very sick, again, with-- also with high fever.
And I really think it was because from the shock, you know?
She got very sick.
And they let us stay there for a few days till she got better.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after that, we went to the Primacs.
And they, of course, couldn't believe it, that I came with-- that I found my sister.
And they said, well, you can stay here with us.
Well, the war was still going on.
It wasn't finished yet, right?
Now, in Korzec, now, from there, they wanted to move to a bigger city, going west, closer to Poland, because now it's Russia, for sure.
The only fighting was, you know, like, I guess, Ukrainian police [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] were looking-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how did it go?
Oh, for Russian soldiers.
Some of them were injured, and they came back.
In fact, one of them did come back on the farm where I was, injured.
And he left early in the morning.
And that same night, Ukrainian police stormed in, and they were looking for Russian soldiers.
In case they were there, they would kill them right away.
And luckily, the soldier wasn't there.
Not during this period.
Before-- I guess, before the war, you know, this kind of pogroms were around for the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Also in this province-- in the same province.
They're in the same province, but going more west to Poland.
They found a place where to stay-- one room apartment or whatever-- the Primacs.
And we stayed with them.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] again, they would go on the market to do business.
Mr.
Primac, in fact, he was sending me to-- to sell some goods.
And one day, I was selling some goods.
I think it was leather, because people were going for leather.
I sold whatever he gave me, and I put my-- the money sort of in a back pocket.
And when I came home, and I told him I sold it, I went to reach for the money, and it was-- wasn't there.
It was stolen.
So I don't think, after that, he sent me to sell-- to sell goods.
And so we were now in Rivne.
And they say, oh, the war's going to be finished soon-- going to be finished soon.
This was in '45.
Well, this was April already.
This was in April-- March, April.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But we knew the war is going to end very soon, and we were free.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, again, one morning-- very early in the morning, we heard loudspeakers outside.
And they're saying, come out.
Come out.
The war is over.
And I think it was, like, May 7 or 8, 1945.
And we all went outside in night or house coats-- whatever everybody was in.
We ran outside.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] loudspeakers were on.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] birds were singing this time.
And bells were ringing.
And everybody was hugging, and big announcement came.
The war is finished.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Not at the time.
I knew there was-- my cousin was hiding.
And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was looking for her at the market to see if-- if anybody I know or my family.
But she didn't show up.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and there's some people-- some friends of my brothers-- were survivors.
Some of my parents'-- Also, I had a cousin in Korzac that didn't survive, no.
Most of them didn't-- very few.
After the end of the war?
Now, we moved from Rivne into Poland, because nobody wants to stay here now.
They want to move west, west.
So now they move to-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to [POLISH] a Polish city, into Poland-- left Ukrainian part-- the Russian.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we went there on a train.
And it was open trains, you know.
And it was raining and pouring.
We were just soaked wet, but we were going-- we were going west.
My sister and I were still with them.

And while we were in [POLISH], then I don't know-- for a short time-- then the Primacs found out that there was an orphanage for young children-- for survivors' children-- in Bielsko, in a different city, not far from [POLISH].
And so my sister went there first.
And I don't know.
They wanted me to stay on with them for awhile in this city, but my sister was in the orphanage first.
And I would visit her.
And so maybe I stayed with them-- probably in '45, maybe the end of '45.
They were going on to Germany now.
And-- and, of course, I said, I'm going to go to the orphanage.
And they didn't say for us to come with them.
So I went, now, to the orphanage.
And they went on to Germany.
And we remained in Poland.
Oh, the conditions were very nice-- very nice personnel.
That's a good question.
Who ran it?
[LAUGHS] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Whether, you mean, it's the Polish government or--
It was a Jewish-- It was a Jewish orphanage, absolutely.
I-- I think New York-- Jewish Congress from New York helped a lot.
I remember Jewish Congress of New  York.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Of course, I was eager to learn in school.
And they sent us to school.
Because we had no schooling, so they put different-- didn't matter what ages, put in one room, one classroom.
And learned Polish-- studied Polish, everything else.
And then after a few months, they sort of put us in different grades, you know, according to age and how much fast you learn.
And I was-- I remember I was so anxious to learn and learned Polish real quick and studying Russian.
And-- and even though it was Poland, but it was communism already, I studied a lot about communism.
Reading books about Karl Marx and Lenin.
And so they were concentrating on that a lot.
I think maybe-- maybe in '47, '48, I think there was a little-- a little Jewish studies.
In the orphanage?
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] But they tried to teach music and dancing in the orphanage.
And--
Singing-- I was in the choir-- in the
My sister, liked-- she was in the piano.
She was-- She just went up to the piano and just played by ear.
And she played beautifully.
Yes.
I meant to say that before, too.
I'm glad you brought it up.
My family was musical.
My brothers played a mandolin.
The mandolin was the musical instrument in the house.
And my brothers and my older sisters played the mandolin.
And I wanted to learn already the mandolin, but I think there was only one mandolin for the family.
And there were lots of songs, lots of songs sang in Yiddish and Russian and Polish, Ukrainian.
And there were always friends-- my brother's friends were always there and sister's friends.
And it was always quite musical.
And here my sister went up to the piano.
And she just started to play piano.
So she had-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she was gifted this way.
And I remember being in the choir.
I used to like singing.
So I would sing.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So she would play the piano, and I would sing.
Outside of Poland?
In the orphanage?
Yes.
Now, I knew I had an aunt in America-- you call it in America, and-- from the Jewish Congress from New York.
They came to the city-- to Bielska.
And I-- it could have been that somebody came to the orphanage.
And they wanted to know if anybody has some relatives in the States.
And I knew I had an aunt.
And I remember Aunt Shaindl, Mimi Shaindl.
And I remembered-- I didn't know her last name, but I remembered her maiden name, which was the same as my mother's maiden name.
And I--
Which was Feldman.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so they told me where to go to show-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to tell the people from New York-- from the Jewish Congress-- about survivors.
And I told somebody in the orphanage.
A personnel lady-- or man, whoever-- took the information, gave them a picture of a me and Susan-- my sister.
And I told them that I have an aunt in the States, and her name is Shaindl Feldman.
And we gave her our names, the survivors, where we're from, everything.
And sure enough, they put it in the Jewish paper-- New York Jewish paper, whatever it was called.
And-- and so what happened-- and my auntie lived in Boston, and she would take the New York Jewish paper-- the Yiddish paper.
And one day, sure enough, she reads in the paper, and she sees our names and our picture.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she got in contact, I guess, with the Jewish Congress where we were.
And she started to write to us.
And that's how we found that first-- about my auntie in Boston.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then the Jewish Congress was very nice, from New York.
And they would send us parcels.
And for some reason, they had my name.
And I would get parcels from them.
And I still remember, there was a bathing suit there.
And all my friends in the orphanage-- it was just one bathing suit-- oh, they were so envious, you know?
Then, in the summer, I put on the bathing suit.
And also, I remember I was getting boxes of oranges-- big oranges.
So of course, when I get the box-- and it was on my name.
And when I get the box of oranges, I would share with all the kids.
My sister, of course, with all the other kids in the orphanage.
And we just-- It was so good.
It was so delicious.
